answers.intake_q1: When did your elbow pain start?
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe what caused the elbow pain or if you injured it in any way?
answers.intake_q3: Can you describe the location of the pain on your elbow and if it is on the inside, outside, or general area?
answers.intake_q4: Any swelling, redness, or warmth around the outside of your elbow?
answers.intake_q5: Any numbness, tingling, or weakness in your arm or hand?
answers.intake_q6: Any difficulty moving your elbow or pain when you try to grip or lift objects?
answers.intake_q7: Any fever, chills, or other signs of infection?
answers.intake_q8: Have you tried any treatments or medications for your elbow pain so far?
answers.intake_q9: Any prior history of elbow problems or repetitive strain injuries?
answers.intake_q10: Any recent trauma or injury to the elbow besides playing tennis?

note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 51-year-old female presenting with a 4-day history of pain localized to the outside of her elbow. The pain began after playing tennis and is associated with some mild swelling but no redness or warmth. She reports pain with lifting objects but denies numbness, tingling, weakness, fever, or chills. She has tried icing the area with some relief. There is no prior history of elbow problems or recent trauma other than the tennis activity. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no physical exam or vital signs available.

A: 
The clinical presentation is consistent with lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow), characterized by pain on the outside of the elbow following repetitive wrist extension activities such as tennis. The mild swelling and pain with lifting support this diagnosis. 

Differential: 
1. Lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow) 
2. Radial tunnel syndrome 
3. Olecranon bursitis 
4. Referred pain from cervical radiculopathy 

P: 
- First-line medication: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen or naproxen for pain and inflammation relief. 
- Alternatives: Acetaminophen if NSAIDs are contraindicated or not tolerated. 
- Medication safety considerations: Use NSAIDs with caution in patients with gastrointestinal, renal, or cardiovascular risk factors; patient currently has no known renal or liver disease. Avoid NSAIDs if allergy develops. 
- Return precautions: Advise patient to seek care if symptoms worsen, if there is increasing swelling, redness, warmth, fever, or if numbness or weakness develops. 
- Testing: Not indicated at this time based on clinical presentation; consider imaging or specialist referral if no improvement after conservative management or if symptoms worsen.
